title: Hummus with warm pitta
subtitle: Smooth chickpea and tahini dip served warm with toasted flatbreads
excerpt: Homemade hummus made from tinned chickpeas blended with tahini, lemon, garlic, and cumin to a very smooth, almost silky consistency. Finished with a pool of good olive oil and a dusting of paprika, served with warm pitta. The difference between homemade and shop-bought is significant.
sourceNotes: Hummus bi tahini is documented in Arabic cookery manuscripts from at least the thirteenth century. The dish is a staple across the Levant, Turkey, Greece, and North Africa. The combination of chickpeas, tahini, lemon, and garlic is the classic and consistent form across the region.

content.text: The silky texture that distinguishes good hummus from average hummus comes from two things: blending for long enough, and the ice water. Add the ice water a tablespoon at a time while the processor is running and the hummus will loosen and become almost white and very smooth. Blending for 3 to 4 minutes rather than 30 seconds makes a noticeable difference.

Some recipes remove the papery skins from the chickpeas for an even smoother result. It takes 10 minutes and produces a perceptibly silkier texture, worth doing if you want restaurant-quality hummus, unnecessary for a good home version. The tahini goes in before the chickpeas and is whipped on its own first, which helps it emulsify better.

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: to finish, plus more to drizzle

content.text: Put the {{tahini}} of tahini and the lemon juice from the {{lemon}} lemons in a food processor. Blend for 1 minute until the tahini lightens in colour and becomes fluffy. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and blend for 30 seconds. Add the {{chickpeas-tinned}} of chickpeas and blend for 1 minute. With the processor running, add the {{ice}} of ice and 2 to 3 tablespoons of the reserved chickpea liquid. Blend for 3 to 4 minutes until very smooth. Taste and adjust the seasoning, add more lemon or salt as needed.

Spoon the hummus onto a wide plate and use the back of a spoon to create a shallow well in the centre. Drizzle over the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Dust with a pinch of {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika. Warm the {{pitta-bread}} pittas in a dry hot pan or oven for 2 minutes. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Blend for at least 3 to 4 minutes in total; add more ice water and scrape down the sides every 30 seconds
items.symptom: Hummus is grainy rather than smooth

items.fix: Start with 1 clove of garlic. Taste before adding the second. Tahini quality varies a lot. Use a runny, pale tahini, not a thick, dark one.
items.cause: Too much raw garlic, or poor-quality tahini

items.fix: Add reserved chickpea liquid or water a tablespoon at a time until the processor runs smoothly and the texture is loose. It will firm up slightly in the fridge
items.symptom: Hummus is too thick to spread

content.text: Hummus bi tahini appears in Arabic cookbooks from the 1200s. It is a staple across the Levant, Turkey, Cyprus, and North Africa. The question of who invented it is hotly debated and probably cannot be settled. Chickpeas and sesame paste have been combined across the eastern Mediterranean for many hundreds of years. The home version, made fresh and eaten the same day, bears little resemblance to the shop-tub kind. Tub hummus is made for long shelf life, not for flavour.
